meta.title: nono PR #856
meta.subject: nono PR #856 — TLS interception for CONNECT routes

scenes.title: TLS interception
scenes.tagline: nono PR #856 — the proxy gains layer-7 reach over HTTPS

beats.narration: A pull request against nono -- the capability-based sandbox for untrusted agents. Pull request eight-five-six.
It closes a real hole in the threat model. An H-T-T-P-S CONNECT tunnel was, until now, an opaque pipe the proxy could not see into. This change makes the proxy a terminating T-L-S endpoint.
Twenty-eight files, nearly four thousand lines added. It closes issue seven-seven-nine.
The heart of the change is a new subsystem in the proxy crate -- tls underscore intercept. It is five files.

A session certificate authority: ephemeral, generated at startup, its private key held only in a zeroizing buffer and never written to disk. And a cert cache that mints a leaf certificate, live, for each host the agent visits.
An acceptor builds the T-L-S server configuration. A bundle composes the trust roots the agent must accept, and writes them to an owner-only file.
And the handle ties it together: it terminates the agent's T-L-S connection, inspects the request inside, and forwards it on.

scenes.heading: Refuse, became inspect

beats.narration: Here is the change that matters, in the proxy's CONNECT handler. Before, a CONNECT to a host nono had a policy route for was simply refused -- a flat four-oh-three. The agent learned nothing, and nono saw nothing.
After, that refusal becomes a decision. If the route asked for layer-seven enforcement, and a certificate cache is available...
...the proxy validates the session token, then calls into the intercept handler -- which terminates the agent's T-L-S connection instead of tunneling blindly past it.
Every other case -- a route with no cache, a declarative route -- still falls through to the old behavior. The change is surgical: it widens exactly one path, and leaves the rest alone.
Watch one intercepted connection. The agent opens a CONNECT tunnel to an H-T-T-P-S host.
The proxy answers two hundred -- connection established -- exactly as an ordinary tunnel would.
But it does not blindly forward. It reads the agent's T-L-S hello, and the host name inside it.
It asks the cert cache for a leaf certificate for that exact host.
The ephemeral session C-A signs one, live. If the agent pins certificates and rejects it, the handshake hard-fails -- the connection is dropped and audited, never silently tunneled.
The handshake completes. The proxy is now a terminating endpoint -- it can read the plaintext request the agent just sent.
It matches the request against the route's rules, injects the managed credential the agent never sees, re-encrypts, and forwards it upstream.
The blast radius. Four new crates enter the dependency graph -- a certificate generator, a zeroizing allocator, native-certificate loading.
Five environment variables are injected into the sandboxed child, so the agent's own tools -- curl, git, Node, Python -- all trust the certificates the proxy will mint.

The audit schema grows -- every proxy event now carries a structured context -- and the reverse-proxy and intercept paths were refactored onto one shared forwarding pipeline. The old reverse path shed four hundred lines.
And the load-bearing safety property: certificate pinning is honored as a hard failure. A pinned client breaks loudly, and is audited. The proxy never quietly degrades into a transparent tunnel.

scenes.heading: PR #856, in four ideas
points: An ephemeral, in-memory CA — minted at startup, never on disk.
CONNECT tunnels to policy routes are terminated, not refused.
Decrypt, filter, inject the credential, re-encrypt, forward.
Certificate pinning hard-fails — the proxy never silently tunnels.

beats.narration: Pull request eight-five-six, in four ideas. An ephemeral, in-memory certificate authority, minted at startup and never written to disk.
CONNECT tunnels to policy routes are now terminated and inspected, not flatly refused.
The proxy decrypts, filters, injects the managed credential, re-encrypts, and forwards.
And certificate pinning hard-fails -- the proxy never silently tunnels past a route that asked for enforcement. A large change, but a careful one: it closes a real hole without opening a new one.
